turns.content: let me ask you something... I have been thinking about this for a few days and I cannot decide. my apprentice Deniz, he showed me something on his phone and now it is stuck in my head
Of course — what did he show you? Sometimes things stick in your head for a reason.

these new barber shops... the fancy ones, you know. they have these menus. not just haircut and shave. they have facials... beard oils, charcoal masks, all these treatments. and they charge 50, 80 lira extra per service on top of everything
I know what you mean — the modern grooming menu trend. The margins on those add-on services can be significant since the product costs are relatively low. What's your gut reaction to it?

my gut says two things at the same time. one part says... Mehmet, this is not what you do. you are a barber. you cut hair, you shave faces, you do the hot towel. Hasan usta did not teach you to put charcoal on a man's face. this is a barbershop, not a spa
That's a completely valid instinct. There's real value in being known for doing a few things exceptionally well. What does the other part say?

the other part... sees the numbers. 50, 80 lira extra per customer. if even 5 customers a day want something extra, that is 250 to 400 lira a day. and with the rent going up... you understand. the numbers talk to me

The math does make sense — even modest uptake would make a real difference monthly. But the question isn't just whether it makes money. It's whether it fits your shop, your hands, and your customers. Do you think your regulars would actually go for it?

the older men who have been coming 20 years... they would look at me like I have lost my mind. 'Mehmet, what is this, a beard oil treatment? just shave me and give me my tea.' but the younger ones... Deniz says they expect it. they go to these new places in Beşiktaş and Nişantaşı and come back smelling like a perfume shop

That's the tension — you'd be serving two different customer bases with different expectations. Have you thought about a middle ground? Like adding one or two things that feel close to what you already do, rather than a full spa menu?

a beard oil treatment... maybe. that is close to what I already do with the hot towel and the razor. just one extra step. that I could do with my hands. the facials and the charcoal... no. I am a barber, not a... Kosmetiker, as they say in German. but I keep saying maybe. part of me thinks I am just an old man afraid of change. and part of me says... 'ayağını yorganına göre uzat'... stretch your foot according to your blanket. do not try to be something you are not

That proverb cuts both ways. It could mean stay in your lane — or it could mean know your resources and use them smartly. Your hands, your experience, your customer relationships — those are your blanket. A beard oil add-on doesn't stretch past that. But honestly, you don't have to decide today.

yes... I am only thinking. that is all. Deniz keeps sending me these Instagram pages from other shops and I look at them at night when Ayşe is watching her shows... and I wonder, could I do this. I do not have the answer yet. maybe next month, maybe next year. we will see

Take your time with it. The fact that you're looking at it seriously rather than dismissing it outright says something. You'll know when you're ready — or you'll know it's not for you. Either answer is fine. The shop has survived 30 years on your instincts.
